clean_story:
Silent decomposers. Underground architects.

"Mushroom" comes from the Old French mousseron, meaning "moss-like"—fitting for organisms that sprout overnight like tiny, fleshy umbrellas. But their real work happens unseen.

A mushroom is just the fruit of a vast, hidden network: mycelium, a web of thread-like cells (hyphae) thinner than spider silk. One cubic inch of soil can hold 8 miles of these threads. They digest dead plants by secreting enzymes—like spraying rust remover on a bike chain, then slurping up the dissolved metal. Without them, forests would choke on their own fallen leaves.

Some mushrooms glow. The jack-o’-lantern mushroom (Omphalotus olearius) pulses green in the dark, luring insects to spread its spores. Others, like armillaria, are among Earth’s largest organisms: a single clone in Oregon covers 2,385 acres—bigger than 1,600 football fields.

Three related subjects are fungal communication networks, bioluminescent life forms, and soil as a living system.
